does: This survey systematizes the application of confidential computing technologies to agentic AI systems, identifying how Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) can defend LLM-driven agents against threats unique to their operational model. The work catalogs six TEE platforms—Intel SGX, Intel TDX, AMD SEV-SNP, ARM TrustZone, ARM CCA, and NVIDIA H100 CC—and maps their capabilities, constraints, and deployment roles. It develops a taxonomy of attack surfaces specific to agents: prompt injection, context exfiltration, credential theft, and inter-agent message poisoning that arise from agent architecture patterns including persistent memory, tool invocation, and peer delegation protocols like MCP and A2A. The survey articulates why software-only defenses prove insufficient against privileged adversaries such as compromised cloud operators, positioning hardware-rooted isolation and remote attestation as necessary complements.
connects: This work bridges two previously separate research streams: agentic AI systems and confidential computing. It extends threat modeling from standalone LLM inference to agent-specific risks introduced by stateful execution, external tool integration, and multi-agent coordination—problems identified in recent agentic AI literature. The survey draws on established TEE research and deployment patterns from datacenter confidential computing, but must address novel constraints imposed by agent workloads: the need to preserve agent agency and tool flexibility while maintaining isolation, the challenge of credential management across distributed agent networks, and attestation protocols suitable for dynamic agent topologies. It synthesizes insights from both LLM security literature (prompt injection, jailbreaking) and systems security (TEE threat models, side-channel attacks), creating connections previously unexplored in formal literature.
enables: This survey establishes a foundation for building genuinely trustworthy agentic AI systems in adversarial environments. It enables practitioners to reason about threat-risk tradeoffs when deploying agents across untrusted infrastructure, and points toward concrete design patterns: agents executing in TEEs to prevent operator snooping, remote attestation protocols for verifying agent integrity to peer systems, and cryptographic credential management within isolated execution contexts. The work creates space for new research on agent-specific TEE primitives—isolation boundaries that protect sensitive context while enabling safe tool delegation, attestation schemes that verify multi-agent consensus without leaking intermediate state, and performance optimization of agent workloads within constrained TEE resources. It also motivates investigation into hybrid defenses combining TEEs with cryptographic techniques, and highlights standards gaps in inter-agent security protocols that confidential computing alone cannot resolve.
